data.title: Cybin stands out as the most undervalued psychedelic stock as Trump signs on faster PTSD research
data.description:
Psychedelic stocks surged in premarket trading on Monday after President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Saturday to dramatically accelerate research into, and expand patient access to psychedelic substances that have shown promise in treating post-traumatic stress disorder and other serious mental illnesses.

The market reaction was swift and broad. Compass Pathways (CMPS) surged 25% in premarket, while AtaiBeckley (ATAI) jumped 22%, Definium Therapeutics (DFTX) climbed 20%, and both GH Research (GHRS) and Cybin (HELP) rose 17%.

Against this backdrop of renewed policy tailwinds, the following list ranks the primary psychedelic sector stocks by their Seeking Alpha valuation grade,  a measure of how cheaply or expensively each stock is priced relative to its fundamentals. An A+ grade indicates the most undervalued, while F signals the most overvalued; a grade of B- or above is generally considered a Buy, while D+ or below is a Sell.

Cybin (HELP) stands out as the only name on the list carrying an outright value signal, earning an A grade and suggesting it may be the most attractively priced relative to fundamentals among its peers. GH Research (GHRS) and Compass Pathways (CMPS) follow with grades of C- and D+, respectively, while Definium Therapeutics (DFTX) and AtaiBeckley (ATAI) both carry D- grades, indicating that despite the premarket enthusiasm, most of these names are trading at a significant premium to their current fundamentals.

Here is the list:

  1. Cybin Inc. (HELP), Valuation grade: A

  2. GH Research PLC (GHRS), Valuation grade: C-

  3. COMPASS Pathways plc (CMPS), Valuation grade: D+

  4. Definium Therapeutics, Inc. (DFTX), Valuation grade: D-

  5. AtaiBeckley Inc. (ATAI), Valuation grade: D-

data.title: TransAlta appoints Mike Politeski as CFO
data.description:
• TransAlta (TAC) has appointed Mike Politeski as executive vice president, finance and chief financial officer, and Grant Arnold as executive vice president, growth and chief commercial officer, effective May 1, 2026, and May 6, 2026, respectively.
  • Politeski has over 25 years of experience and, most recently, served as senior vice president, finance & treasurer at Veren Inc. (previously Crescent Point Energy).
  • Arnold also brings over 30 years of experience in the power generation and energy sector. He served as president and CEO of BluEarth Renewables and held senior roles at Suncor Energy previously.
  • The appointments correspond with the transition of current EVP and CFO, Joel Hunter, to the role of president and chief executive officer effective April 30, 2026, following the retirement of current President and CEO, John Kousinioris.

data.title: Stablecoins raise regulatory evasion, dollarization risks - BIS head
data.description:
Wider adoption of stablecoins in their current form would raise risks for financial integrity, regulatory evasion and increased dollarization, the head of the Bank for International Settlements has warned.

During a Bank of Japan seminar on Monday, BIS General Manager Pablo Hernández de Cos said enhanced design arrangements and robust liquidity backstops would be needed for stablecoins to play a more meaningful role in the financial system.

According to de Cos, stablecoins raise serious risks for financial integrity and can facilitate regulatory circumvention, increasing dollarization risks for emerging markets. Currently, about 98% of stablecoins are U.S.-dollar denominated.

Tobias Adrian, director of the IMF's monetary and capital markets department, told the Financial Times that the major challenge when it comes to stablecoins is dollarization. "For the central bank, it may be a threat to monetary sovereignty."

BIS' de Cos also emphasized the need for international cooperation on stablecoins, without which "divergent regulatory frameworks for stablecoins across jurisdictions could lead to severe market fragmentation or enable harmful regulatory arbitrage."

The strongest growth in the industry has come from Tether's USDT (USDT-USD), which does not comply with the stablecoin laws of major jurisdictions, de Cos said.

"Tether (USDT-USD) and Circle (CRCL) exhibit features that make them resemble securities rather than money," he said. "In particular, they impose redemption frictions, leading to frequent deviations from par in secondary markets. In this respect, they currently operate more like exchange-traded funds than like money."

Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey also voiced concerns over stablecoin regulation last week, as "the rate of progress" on developing international rules had slowed.

data.title: enCore Energy names Little as new CEO; Sheriff returns as Executive Chair
data.description:
enCore Energy (EU) said Monday it appointed Richard Little as its CEO, replacing Robert Willette, effective immediately, and enCore founder and former Executive Chair William Sheriff will return immediately as Executive Chair.

Willette became enCore's (EU) CEO just last September after spending six months as acting CEO following 20-years as an accomplished general counsel and business executive; he had previously served as ProFrac's chief legal officer, chief compliance officer, and corporate secretary.

Little previously served as CEO of Fury Resources, Ajax Resources—where he engineered the sale of substantially all of its assets in the Northern Midland Basin to Diamondback Energy in a $1.2B deal—and Halcon Resources.

data.title: Ingles Markets sends a letter to shareholders amid its proxy fight
data.description:
The board of directors of Ingles Markets (IMKTA) sent an open letter to shareholders related to its proxy fight. Ahead of the company's annual meeting on April 30, the company urged shareholders to vote for its two nominees, Rebekah Lowe and Dwight Jacobs. The board said the vote is critical because it is trying to block Rory Held, the nominee backed by Summer Road LLC, which Ingles (IMKTA) says is tied to the Sackler family and would create conflicted loyalties and value risk for the company.

In the letter, Ingles Markets (IMKTA) argued that it has been recovering well from Hurricane Helene and that its business momentum is improving. It pointed to stronger FQ1 results, including higher sales, same-store sales, gross profit, net income, and EBITDA. The balance sheet was said to remain strong. Ingles also says it believes its strategy of investing in stores, technology, equipment, real estate, and customer service has helped deliver strong long-term shareholder returns.

The company’s main objection to Held is not just that he would represent an activist investor, but that  his ties to Sackler-controlled trusts and entities could create divided fiduciary duties. Ingles (IMKTA) argued that those ties would hurt customer trust, pharmacy sales, fuel sales, and ultimately the company’s reputation and long-term value. It also warned that Summer Road and Held are pushing a financial engineer agenda that stresses quick-hit gains at the expense of long-term value creation.

Shares of Ingles Markets (IMKTA) are up 47% over the last 52 weeks. Short interest on the grocery stock stands at 6.7% of the total float.

data.title: Costco and FedEx face lawsuits as tariff refund portal opens today
data.description:
Billions of dollars are expected to be refunded to American importers after the U.S. Supreme Court struck down President Trump's IEEPA tariffs in February, but the refunds won't be automatic. Businesses will need to apply through a new system set to launch today called CAPE, or the Consolidated Administration and Processing of Entries. Around 330K importers across the nation are likely to be eligible, along with their 53M in shipments that came in between early 2025 and February 2026.

Administrative process: Claims will be submitted by importers or authorized brokers through U.S. Customs and Border Protection's ACE Portal, with an estimated $166-$175B in past tariffs eligible for repayment. Phase 1 of the new system focuses on simpler cases, like recent imports and straightforward claims, with more complex cases coming later, such as older or disputed entries. The whole process will take time to sort out, but Customs and Border Protection has promised to get refunds out via electronic transfers in 60 to 90 days.

The trade fight still continues, with the Trump administration seeking to get IEEPA tariffs reinstated under a different designation. Use of Section 122 and 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, as well as Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, is being sought to increase leverage in trade negotiations. Last week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that by the beginning of July, tariffs may be restored to the levels in place before the Supreme Court invalidated many of the levies.

What about the consumer? While many businesses absorbed the cost of the tariffs, others passed them on to customers, who have no method to get refunded. However, some lawsuits have been filed, like recent proposed class actions against Costco (COST) and FedEx (FDX), arguing that the firms stand to benefit from a federal government refund system that should ultimately be returned to consumers. The companies have taken differing approaches in their response, with FedEx (FDX) saying it would give its tariff refunds to customers, while Costco (COST) intends to return the value to members through lower prices and better value in the future.

Seeking Alpha survey: As the U.S. prepares to refund billions of dollars in tariffs, what should companies do with the money that they collect? Take the poll here and share how it might continue to impact markets in the comments section.

data.title: The oil shock that 'dwarfs history' won't have a rapid recovery - SocGen
data.description:
The scale of the current oil disruption exceeds past crises, with recovery likely to be slow and uneven rather than swift, according to Société Générale.

Dr. Mike Haigh, head of FIC and commodity, argued that while historical oil shocks -- from the Iranian revolution to the Iran-Iraq war -- eventually saw supply replaced or demand adjust, the present disruption is constrained less by production capacity and more by logistics, geopolitics and financial frictions.

A key issue is the closure of critical transit routes, particularly the Strait of Hormuz, which Société Générale estimates removed roughly 10M barrels per day of supply in March alone -- equivalent to a significant share of global output.

Unlike prior shocks, where alternative producers could ramp up output relatively quickly, the current bottleneck lies in moving oil rather than extracting it. The bank highlighted that disrupted shipping flows, longer transit times and the withdrawal of war-risk insurance are slowing the reallocation of supply across regions, creating persistent imbalances.

Historically, such disruptions have taken time to unwind. Société Générale noted that significant Middle East oil supply shocks since 1956 have required around eight months on average to normalize, reinforcing its view that expectations for a rapid recovery are overly optimistic. The bank now expects the current disruption -- described as the largest supply shock on record -- to normalize only by the end of 2026.

 

 

As a result, the shock is propagating through the global system in stages rather than all at once. Cargoes can take anywhere from 10 days to reach India to as long as 40 days to reach the U.S. Gulf Coast, meaning shortages emerge sequentially across markets.

This staggered impact has already led to sharp regional divergences. OECD countries have absorbed much of the immediate strain through large inventory drawdowns and strategic petroleum reserve releases, while emerging markets -- particularly in Asia -- have faced tighter product availability as replacement supplies lag.

Even under a best-case scenario where disruptions ease quickly, Société Générale said global inventories are unlikely to begin normalizing until late May at the earliest, underscoring the delayed nature of the adjustment process.

Reflecting these dynamics, the bank has already revised its near-term outlook. Its base case now assumes a slower normalization of Persian Gulf flows by mid-May rather than late April, a shift that lifts its year-end Brent crude (CO1:COM) (BNO) forecast to $85/bbl from $79/bbl.

Even that higher forecast may prove conservative. Société Générale warned that the scale of shut-ins, combined with constraints around shipping capacity, insurance availability, port damage and debris clearance, could extend the disruption further than currently anticipated.

The bank contrasted this with earlier episodes such as the 1980–81 Iran-Iraq war, when alternative supply came online within months and demand eventually adjusted through efficiency gains and fuel switching.

This time, however, the constraints are more complex and system-wide. The combination of disrupted logistics, insurance frictions and uneven inventory distribution suggests that even if production recovers, the path back to equilibrium will be prolonged.

Novo posts late-stage trial win for sickle cell disease therapy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO) announced on Monday that its HIBISCUS pivotal Phase 3 trial for etavopivat, an experimental therapy for sickle cell disease ((SCD)), reached both co-primary endpoints, and the company plans to seek regulatory approval for the drug this year.

According to topline data, the 52-week trial for 385 people aged 12 years and above with SCD reached co-primary endpoints, as etavopivat, in comparison to placebo, led to a greater reduction in vaso-occlusive crises and a greater improvement in hemoglobin ((Hb)) response.

The oral, once-daily therapy also indicated a safety profile consistent with its prior studies, Novo (NONOF) said, adding that in an exploratory analysis, etavopivat significantly cut the risk of blood transfusion.

The company plans to submit HIBISCUS data, seeking the first regulatory nod for etavopivat in H2 2026, and present the detailed results from the trial at a future medical event.
